title: Named Entity Recognition: The ‘Who, What, Where’ of NLP
text:
Published by Ramu Nalla - January 20, 2026
Imagine you are a bank analyst. Every morning, you receive 10,000 news articles about the stock market. Somewhere in that mountain of text is a sentence saying: “Apple Inc. is acquiring a startup in Berlin for $50 million.”
To a human, the important facts jump out immediately:
To a computer, that sentence is just a string of 62 meaningless characters.
Named Entity Recognition (NER) is the sub-field of NLP that bridges this gap. It is the task of locating and classifying specific entities in unstructured text into predefined categories.
In this post, I will strip away the complexity of modern Large Language Models and go back to basics. We will understand how NER works under the hood and build a simple, functional NER model using raw PyTorch.

title: Named Entity Recognition: The ‘Who, What, Where’ of NLP
text:
Why Do We Need NER?
NER is rarely the “final product.” It is usually the first step in a larger pipeline. It turns unstructured chaos into structured tables.

1. Customer Support Routing
If a user tweets: “My iPhone 14 screen cracked and I need a repair in Chicago,” an NER system detects:

Product: iPhone 14
Issue: Screen cracked
Location: Chicago

The system can then automatically route this ticket to the “Hardware Repair Team” in the “Illinois Region,” saving hours of manual sorting.


2. Algorithmic Trading
Financial bots scrape news feeds milliseconds after they are published. “Tesla (TSLA) announces battery factory expansion in Texas.” The bot identifies ORG: Tesla and LOC: Texas, cross-references this with sentiment analysis, and executes a trade before a human trader has even sipped their coffee.


3. Healthcare (Clinical NER)
Doctors write messy notes. “Patient prescribed 50mg Metoprolol daily.” NER extracts Drug: Metoprolol and Dosage: 50mg to automatically check for dangerous interactions with other drugs the patient is taking.

title: Named Entity Recognition: The ‘Who, What, Where’ of NLP
text:
How it Works: The “BIO” Scheme
NER is technically a Token Classification task. We don’t classify the whole sentence; we classify every single word.
But how do we handle multi-word entities like “New York City”? We can’t just tag “New” as Location, “York” as Location, and “City” as Location, because the model might think they are three separate cities.
We use the BIO tagging scheme:

B-XXX (Begin): The first token of an entity.
I-XXX (Inside): Subsequent tokens of the same entity.
O (Outside): Not an entity.

Example: “Steve Jobs founded Apple”



Word
Tag
Meaning




Steve
B-PER
Beginning of Person


Jobs
I-PER
Inside of Person


founded
O
Outside


Apple
B-ORG
Beginning of Organization

title: Named Entity Recognition: The ‘Who, What, Where’ of NLP
section: Building a Simple NER Model in PyTorch
text:
Building a Simple NER Model in PyTorch
We will build a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network. While Transformers are the state-of-the-art, LSTMs remain excellent for understanding the sequential nature of NER (e.g., seeing “New” makes “York” more likely to be a location).

1. The Setup
First, let’s define our “vocabulary” (words the model knows) and our “tag set” (labels we want to predict).
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim

# A tiny dummy dataset
# Sentence: "Apple is looking at buying U.K. startup for $1 billion"
training_data = [
    (
        "Apple is looking at buying U.K. startup for $1 billion".split(),
        ["B-ORG", "O", "O", "O", "O", "B-LOC", "O", "O", "B-MONEY", "I-MONEY", "I-MONEY"]
    ),
    (
        "Steve Jobs founded Apple".split(),
        ["B-PER", "I-PER", "O", "B-ORG"]
    )
]

# Create mappings (Word -> ID, Tag -> ID)
word_to_ix = {}
for sent, tags in training_data:
    for word in sent:
        if word not in word_to_ix:
            word_to_ix[word] = len(word_to_ix)

tag_to_ix = {
    "O": 0, 
    "B-ORG": 1, "I-ORG": 2, 
    "B-PER": 3, "I-PER": 4, 
    "B-LOC": 5, "I-LOC": 6,
    "B-MONEY": 7, "I-MONEY": 8
}


2. The Model Architecture
Our model will have three layers:

Embedding Layer: Converts word IDs into dense vectors.
LSTM Layer: Reads the sequence and captures context (history).
Linear (Hidden) Layer: Projects the LSTM output to the number of tags.

class NERModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, vocab_size, embedding_dim, hidden_dim, tagset_size):
        super(NERModel, self).__init__()
        self.hidden_dim = hidden_dim

        # 1. Word Embeddings
        self.word_embeddings = nn.Embedding(vocab_size, embedding_dim)

        # 2. LSTM
        # Input: Embedding Vector
        # Output: Hidden Vector (Context)
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(embedding_dim, hidden_dim)

        # 3. Output Layer
        # Maps hidden state -> Tag probability scores
        self.hidden2tag = nn.Linear(hidden_dim, tagset_size)

    def forward(self, sentence):
        # Step 1: Embed the words
        # Shape: (Seq_Len) -> (Seq_Len, Embedding_Dim)
        embeds = self.word_embeddings(sentence)
        
        # Step 2: Run LSTM
        # view(len, 1, -1) adds a batch dimension of 1 because LSTM expects (Seq, Batch, Dim)
        lstm_out, _ = self.lstm(embeds.view(len(sentence), 1, -1))
        
        # Step 3: Project to Tag Space
        # Shape: (Seq_Len, Tagset_Size)
        tag_space = self.hidden2tag(lstm_out.view(len(sentence), -1))
        
        # Step 4: Softmax (Log Softmax for stability)
        tag_scores = nn.functional.log_softmax(tag_space, dim=1)
        
        return tag_scores


3. Training the Model
We use Negative Log Likelihood Loss (NLLLoss) because we are doing multi-class classification for each word. We’ll set up a standard training loop using Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD).
# Hyperparameters
EMBEDDING_DIM = 6
HIDDEN_DIM = 6

# Initialize model
model = NERModel(len(word_to_ix), EMBEDDING_DIM, HIDDEN_DIM, len(tag_to_ix))
loss_function = nn.NLLLoss()
optimizer = optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.1)

# Helper to convert sentence to indices
def prepare_sequence(seq, to_ix):
    idxs = [to_ix[w] for w in seq]
    return torch.tensor(idxs, dtype=torch.long)

# Training Loop
for epoch in range(300): 
    for sentence, tags in training_data:
        # 1. Clear gradients
        model.zero_grad()

        # 2. Prepare inputs
        sentence_in = prepare_sequence(sentence, word_to_ix)
        targets = prepare_sequence(tags, tag_to_ix)

        # 3. Forward pass
        tag_scores = model(sentence_in)

        # 4. Calculate Loss & Backprop
        loss = loss_function(tag_scores, targets)
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()


4. Testing the Model
Let’s see if the model memorized our simple examples. We will pass a test sentence through the trained model and decode the indices back into readable tags.
with torch.no_grad():
    # Test sentence: "Steve Jobs founded Apple"
    inputs = prepare_sequence(training_data[1][0], word_to_ix)
    tag_scores = model(inputs)
    
    # Get the index of the highest score for each word
    _, predicted_indices = torch.max(tag_scores, 1)
    
    # Convert indices back to tag names
    ix_to_tag = {v: k for k, v in tag_to_ix.items()}
    predicted_tags = [ix_to_tag[idx.item()] for idx in predicted_indices]

    print(f"Sentence: {training_data[1][0]}")
    print(f"Predicted Tags: {predicted_tags}")

# Output:
# Sentence: ['Steve', 'Jobs', 'founded', 'Apple']
# Predicted Tags: ['B-PER', 'I-PER', 'O', 'B-ORG']

title: Named Entity Recognition: The ‘Who, What, Where’ of NLP
text:
Understanding the Tensor Shapes
This is where most people get stuck in PyTorch. Let’s trace the shapes for the sentence “Steve Jobs founded Apple” (Length 4).

Input: [ID_Steve, ID_Jobs, ...] \(\rightarrow\) Shape (4)
Embedding: Each ID becomes a vector of size 6. \(\rightarrow\) Shape (4, 6)
LSTM Input: PyTorch LSTMs expect 3D inputs: (Seq_Len, Batch, Dim). We reshape. \(\rightarrow\) Shape (4, 1, 6)
LSTM Output: Returns vectors of hidden_dim (6). \(\rightarrow\) Shape (4, 1, 6)
Linear Layer: We squash the batch dimension to feed into the Linear layer. Input (4, 6). Output is (4, num_tags).

For every word (4 words), we get 9 scores (one for each tag in our tagset).

title: Named Entity Recognition: The ‘Who, What, Where’ of NLP
text:
Summary
Named Entity Recognition is about context. The word “Apple” is a fruit if “ate” is nearby, but an Organization if “founded” is nearby.
By using an LSTM (or Transformer), we allow the model to carry this context across the sentence. While our example was tiny, modern NER systems scaling this up can read millions of documents a day, structuring the world’s information one entity at a time.

title: Deliberate-SLM: System 2 Reasoning and Production efficiency
text:
Published by Ramu Nalla - May 11, 2026
In the landscape of 2026, the gap between “Small Language Models” (SLMs) and “Frontier Reasoners” (like OpenAI’s o1 or DeepSeek-R1) is no longer just about parameter count—it is about thinking time.
Standard SLMs lean on “System 1” thinking: they are instinctive and fast, but they often hallucinate on logic because they try to compress the whole answer into one forward pass.
To address that, I built Deliberate-SLM—not just another fine-tune, but a multi-stage alignment pipeline that:
On my benchmark suite, that combination delivered real numbers: oracle accuracy climbed from 55% to 85%—a 30-point absolute gain—by scaling test-time compute with Best-of-N before shrinking the habit down to Chain-of-Draft, which cut average latency from 17.4s to 4.5s (3.8×) and thought tokens from 234 to 60 (~74% savings) with 96.2% accuracy retention versus the full internal-monologue baseline. The sections below walk through how each stage earned those outcomes.

title: Deliberate-SLM: System 2 Reasoning and Production efficiency
text:
The Cognitive Gap
The first challenge was data. Standard Chain-of-Thought (CoT) datasets are linear—they show a straight path to the answer. But true reasoning is messy; it involves backtracking and second-guessing.
I architected a Teacher-Student Distillation pipeline using Llama-3.3-70B as the “Gold Standard” teacher. Instead of a standard prompt, I utilized a Reflective System Prompt that forced the teacher to use [reflection] tags whenever it hit a complex logical junction.
To ensure the “distillation signal” was pure, I developed a Rationale Verifier. This script algorithmically compared the teacher’s final <answer> to the ground truth. If the teacher arrived at the right answer through flawed logic, the sample was purged. This resulted in a high-fidelity dataset of 600+ “Verified Rationales” where the model explicitly double-checks its own work.

title: Deliberate-SLM: System 2 Reasoning and Production efficiency
section: Teaching the Habit: QLoRA Fine-Tuning with Unsloth
text:
Teaching the Habit: QLoRA Fine-Tuning with Unsloth
Training a 3B model on a consumer-grade T4 GPU (16GB VRAM) is a memory minefield. To solve this, I chose Unsloth as the backbone of the SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning) phase.

Why Unsloth?
Standard Hugging Face SFTTrainer implementations are often memory-inefficient because they don’t optimize the backpropagation math for specific architectures. Unsloth patches the internal kernels of the model (in this case, Qwen2.5-3B), making the training 2x faster and reducing VRAM consumption by 70%. This allowed me to target all linear layers (q, k, v, o, gate, up, down) for fine-tuning—a necessity for capturing the nuanced weights associated with logical backtracking.
# The Unsloth/TRL SFT Configuration used to bake in reasoning
trainer = SFTTrainer(
    model = model,
    tokenizer = tokenizer,
    train_dataset = dataset,
    dataset_text_field = "text",
    max_seq_length = 2048,
    packing = True, # Efficiency optimization to group short sequences
    args = SFTConfig(
        per_device_train_batch_size = 2,
        gradient_accumulation_steps = 4,
        max_steps = 60, 
        learning_rate = 2e-4,
        optim = "adamw_8bit", # Saves 2GB+ VRAM
        packing_strategy = "bfd", # Best-Fit-Decreasing for optimal token throughput
    ),
)
By the end of this phase, the student model had internalized the “thinking habit.” It no longer needed to be prompted to think; its most likely next-token prediction after a question was now naturally a <think> tag.

title: Deliberate-SLM: System 2 Reasoning and Production efficiency
text:
Test-Time Compute Scaling
Once the model knew how to think, the next hurdle was thinking enough. Borrowing from the “o1” scaling laws, I implemented Inference-Time Scaling via a Best-of-N (BoN) pipeline.
The core idea: If the model’s first reasoning path is wrong, its fourth or fifth might be right. I engineered a verifier-loop that generates 5 distinct reasoning trajectories. An LLM-as-a-Judge verifier then scores these paths, looking for logical consistency and self-correction.
The results were a definitive proof of the Inference Scaling Law: Accuracy increased linearly as I scaled the “thinking budget” (N).



Inference-Time Scaling Curve: Accuracy gain vs Number of Thinking Paths (N)


As shown in the plot, the model achieved a 30% absolute accuracy gain (from 55% to 85% Oracle accuracy) simply by allowing the model more “tokens to deliberate” at runtime.

title: Deliberate-SLM: System 2 Reasoning and Production efficiency
text:
Production Reality: Chain-of-Draft (CoD) Efficiency
In a production environment, 200 tokens of “internal monologue” per query is a latency disaster. To make this project “Production-Ready,” I implemented Chain-of-Draft (CoD) Distillation.
I hypothesized that once the model understands the logic, it doesn’t need full sentences. I used a “Compressor Teacher” to rewrite the long CoT rationales into minimalist shorthand sketches (e.g., 50*0.2=10 -> 50-10=40 instead of a 30-word sentence).
I then fine-tuned a second version of the model on this compressed data. This created an “Efficiency-First” model that reasons in symbols rather than prose.

The Latency vs. Intelligence Trade-off
I developed a custom benchmarking suite to measure the impact of this optimization. By implementing a custom Multi-Token Stopping Criteria (forcing the GPU to kill generation the moment </answer> appears), I finally achieved the “Killer” production metrics.










Metric
Full Reasoning (Standard)
Chain-of-Draft (Optimized)
Impact




Avg. Latency
17.36s
4.53s
3.8x Speedup


Avg. Thought Tokens
234

74.3% Savings


Accuracy Retention
100%
96.2%

title: Deliberate-SLM: System 2 Reasoning and Production efficiency
section: Conclusion: The New Frontier of SLMs
text:
Conclusion: The New Frontier of SLMs
The Deliberate-SLM project proves that SLMs are far more capable than we give them credit for. By moving reasoning from a “prompting trick” to a “modeling habit,” and then optimizing that habit for production speed, we can build edge-deployable models that rival the logic of 70B+ parameter giants.
The final artifacts—including the Reasoning Model and the CoD Optimized Model—are now live on Hugging Face, serving as a blueprint for efficient, high-fidelity alignment.
You can explore the full codebase, including the Rationale Verifier and the Best-of-N scaling scripts, in the deliberate-slm repository on GitHub.

title: Building a Minimal RAG Pipeline from Scratch
text:
Published by Ramu Nalla - December 17, 2025
If you have worked with Large Language Models (LLMs) for more than five minutes, you have likely run into their biggest flaw: hallucinations. They sound confident, but they don’t know your private data, and their knowledge cutoff is always in the past.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is the industry standard solution to this. It bridges the gap between a “frozen” LLM and your dynamic, private data.
Before I started using complex vector databases like Pinecone or Milvus, I wanted to understand exactly what was happening under the hood. So, I built a minimal RAG pipeline using nothing but Python and NumPy.

title: Building a Minimal RAG Pipeline from Scratch
text:
The Core Concept
RAG isn’t a single algorithm; it’s a workflow.

Retrieval: Find the most relevant documents for a user’s question.
Augmentation: Paste those documents into the LLM’s prompt context.
Generation: Ask the LLM to answer the question using only that context.

The “magic” happens in step 1. How does a computer know that “neural network” and “deep learning” are related? The answer is Vector Embeddings.

title: Building a Minimal RAG Pipeline from Scratch
section: The Code: “Vector Search” in Pure Python
text:
The Code: “Vector Search” in Pure Python
In a real production environment, we use embedding models (like OpenAI’s text-embedding-3-small) to turn text into massive lists of numbers. For this demo, I’ll manually create small 3-dimensional vectors to visualize the math.
Here is the entire logic in one script:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity

# 1. Our "Knowledge Base"
# In reality, this would be thousands of PDF chunks stored in a Vector DB.
documents = [
    "RAG bridges the gap between LLMs and private data.",
    "LangChain is a popular framework for building AI agents.",
    "Vector databases like Pinecone store embeddings for fast retrieval."
]

# 2. Mock Embeddings
# Imagine these are the output of an embedding model.
# Notice how Doc 1 (0.9, 0.1, ...) and Doc 3 (0.1, 0.2, ...) are mathematically different.
doc_vectors = np.array([
    [0.9, 0.1, 0.1],  # Embedding for Document 1
    [0.2, 0.8, 0.2],  # Embedding for Document 2
    [0.1, 0.2, 0.9]   # Embedding for Document 3
])

# 3. The User's Query
# "How do I use private data with LLMs?" 
# This query is semantically similar to Document 1.
query_vector = np.array([[0.85, 0.15, 0.1]]) 

# 4. The Retrieval Step (Cosine Similarity)
# We calculate the angle between the Query and every Document.
# Higher score = Closer match.
scores = cosine_similarity(query_vector, doc_vectors).flatten()

# 5. Get the Winner
best_match_idx = np.argmax(scores)
retrieved_doc = documents[best_match_idx]
confidence = scores[best_match_idx]

print(f"✅ User Query Mapped to: '{retrieved_doc}'")
print(f"📊 Confidence Score: {confidence:.4f}")

What just happened?
When we ran cosine_similarity, Python calculated the angle between our query vector and the document vectors.

The query vector [0.85, 0.15, 0.1] was heavily weighted towards the first dimension.
Document 1 [0.9, 0.1, 0.1] was also weighted towards the first dimension.
Therefore, the math says: “These two ideas are related.”

title: Building a Minimal RAG Pipeline from Scratch
text:
Moving to Production
While this numpy example is great for intuition, it doesn’t scale to millions of rows. In my professional work, moving from this proof-of-concept to production involves:

Real Embeddings: Replacing manual vectors with sentence-transformers or OpenAI embeddings.
Vector Store: Using a dedicated database (ChromaDB, Weaviate, or Snowflake) to index millions of vectors.
Reranking: Adding a second step to double-check the relevance of the retrieved documents.

I’ll be writing more about building Agentic workflows on top of this RAG architecture in future posts. Stay tuned!

title: Decoding RoPE: Rotary Positional Embeddings
text:
Published by Ramu Nalla - April 17, 2026
Transformers are fundamentally order-agnostic. If you do not explicitly inject positional information into the tokens, a Self-Attention layer will treat the sentence “The dog bit the man” exactly the same as “The man bit the dog.”
Historically, we solved this by adding a positional vector to the token embedding (\(x + p\)). But as we pushed context windows from 512 tokens to 128K and beyond, absolute positioning mathematically collapsed.
Today, almost every State-of-the-Art model (Llama 3, Mistral, Gemma) uses Rotary Positional Embeddings (RoPE). Here is a technical breakdown of why absolute encodings failed, the dimension-agnostic elegance of complex-plane rotations, and the exact mathematical trick used to scale LLMs to 1 Million+ tokens.

title: Decoding RoPE: Rotary Positional Embeddings
text:
The Flaw of Absolute Positioning
The original Transformer paper utilized sinusoidal absolute positional encodings.

The Entanglement Problem: A static positional vector (\(p\)) is added to the word embedding (\(x\)). Once added, the position is permanently entangled with the semantic meaning.
The Attention Bottleneck: When computing the Attention matrix (\(Q \times K^T\)), the model is forced to process four separate dot products:

Query Concept \(\times\) Key Concept (What we actually want)
Query Concept \(\times\) Key Position (Noise)
Query Position \(\times\) Key Concept (Noise)
Query Position \(\times\) Key Position (Absolute distance, but heavily diluted)


We don’t actually care if the word “Data” is at index 14 and “Scientist” is at index 15. We only care that their relative distance is 1. We needed a way to inject position into the dot product directly, without mutating the base semantic embedding.

Depth-Induced Signal Dilution (The Washout Effect)
There is a more insidious architectural flaw with absolute encodings: they are only injected once, at Layer 0.

The Residual Washout: As the tensor (\(x + p\)) propagates through 30, 50, or 100+ transformer blocks, it is subjected to repeated non-linear transformations via MLPs and attention-weighted mixing.
Geometric Degradation: Every time the tensor passes through a dense layer, the original pure geometric signal (\(p\)) is transformed and diluted. By the time the tensor reaches the deeper reasoning layers, the network has largely “forgotten” the exact positional coordinates.

title: Decoding RoPE: Rotary Positional Embeddings
text:
RoPE: The Complex Plane Paradigm
Instead of adding a vector at the embedding layer, RoPE rotates the vector directly inside the attention mechanism at every single layer.
By treating the dimensions of an embedding as coordinates in a 2D complex plane, RoPE applies a rotation matrix parameterized by the token’s absolute position.

The Mathematical Invariant
The genius of RoPE lies in a simple property of vector algebra: If you rotate two vectors by angles \(\theta_1\) and \(\theta_2\), the dot product between them becomes a strict function of their angle difference (\(\theta_1 - \theta_2\)).
When we rotate a Query vector at position \(m\) and a Key vector at position \(n\), their resulting dot product is entirely dependent on their relative distance (\(m - n\)):
\[<f_q(x_m, m), f_k(x_n, n)> = g(x_m, x_n, m-n)\]


The Euler Operation & Dimension Agnosticism
To achieve this computationally, RoPE relies on Euler’s formula.
\[f(q, m) = q e^{im\theta}\]

\(q\): The Query or Key vector.
\(m\): The absolute position index of the token.
\(\theta\): The pre-defined base rotation frequency.

A massive advantage here is that RoPE is strictly dimension-agnostic. Absolute encodings must be explicitly mapped to the d_model size. RoPE, however, operates exclusively on 2D pairs. It takes Dimension 0 and 1 as an \((x, y)\) coordinate, then Dimension 2 and 3, and so on. Whether your attention head dimension is 64, 128, or 256, RoPE just applies the rotation to successive 2D slices sequentially, making it inherently flexible across custom architectures.

title: Decoding RoPE: Rotary Positional Embeddings
text:
Matrix Mechanics: Wavelengths and Memory
We do not rotate every 2D pair by the same angle. If we did, the model would lose track of order in long sequences. Instead, the base frequency \(\theta\) decays exponentially across the dimensions.

High-Frequency Dimensions (Local Context): The first pair of dimensions spins rapidly. A shift of just one or two tokens dramatically changes their rotation angle. This allows the model to precisely differentiate between immediate neighbors (e.g., “not good” vs “good not”).
Low-Frequency Dimensions (Global Context): The final pairs of dimensions spin incredibly slowly. Their “wavelength” is massive. They act as a low-frequency anchor, allowing the model to understand macro-structures, like whether a token belongs to the introduction or the conclusion of a 50-page document.

title: Decoding RoPE: Rotary Positional Embeddings
section: The Secret to 1M Context Windows: Tuning \(\theta\)
text:
The Secret to 1M Context Windows: Tuning \(\theta\)
If RoPE is so robust, why do models hallucinate when you feed them a document longer than their training length?
If a model was trained on 8K tokens, the rotation angles for tokens at position 9,000+ are completely “out of distribution” (OOD). The model has never seen the vector “clock hands” spun that far, resulting in catastrophic attention collapse.
The solution that unlocked 128K and 1M+ context windows (used in techniques like YaRN and Dynamic NTK-Aware Scaling) lies entirely in the base frequency parameter: \(\theta\).

Squeezing the Frequencies
In the original paper, the base frequency was set to \(\theta = 10,000\). If we want to extend a model from an 8K context to a 128K context without retraining from scratch, we simply increase the base frequency (e.g., \(\theta = 500,000\) or even \(50,000,000\)).

The Math: By increasing the denominator in the frequency calculation, we effectively slow down the rotation of the entire spectrum.
The Result: Instead of the vectors spinning out of bounds, we “squeeze” the rotations. The angles that used to represent 128,000 tokens are mathematically compressed to fit within the 8,000-token rotational space the model already understands. It essentially trades micro-resolution for macro-capacity.

title: Decoding RoPE: Rotary Positional Embeddings
text:
Implementing the Rotation (PyTorch)
To implement this efficiently on GPUs, we bypass standard rotation matrices and utilize PyTorch’s native complex number support. Notice the theta parameter in the precomputation step.

1. Precomputing the Frequencies
We calculate the rotation angles once during initialization to avoid overhead during the forward pass.
import torch

def precompute_freqs_cis(dim: int, seq_len: int, theta: float = 10000.0) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Precomputes the complex exponential rotation frequencies."""
    
    # 1. Calculate frequency decay across dimensions: 1 / (theta ^ (2i / dim))
    # Note: Increasing 'theta' here is how we scale to 1M+ context windows.
    freqs = 1.0 / (theta ** (torch.arange(0, dim, 2)[: (dim // 2)].float() / dim))
    
    # 2. Create sequence positions: [0, 1, ..., seq_len-1]
    t = torch.arange(seq_len, device=freqs.device, dtype=torch.float32)
    
    # 3. Outer product yields the specific angle for each position & dimension pair
    freqs = torch.outer(t, freqs).float()
    
    # 4. Convert angles to complex numbers: cos(freqs) + i * sin(freqs)
    freqs_cis = torch.polar(torch.ones_like(freqs), freqs)
    return freqs_cis


2. Applying the Rotation
During the forward pass, immediately after projecting the \(Q\) and \(K\) tensors (and crucially, before the dot product), we execute the rotation.
def apply_rotary_emb(xq: torch.Tensor, xk: torch.Tensor, freqs_cis: torch.Tensor):
    """Physically rotates Q and K vectors in the complex plane."""
    
    # 1. Reshape last dimension into coordinate pairs and cast to complex
    xq_ = torch.view_as_complex(xq.float().reshape(*xq.shape[:-1], -1, 2))
    xk_ = torch.view_as_complex(xk.float().reshape(*xk.shape[:-1], -1, 2))
    
    # 2. Reshape precomputed frequencies to match batch and head dimensions
    ndim = xq_.ndim
    shape = [d if i == 1 or i == ndim - 1 else 1 for i, d in enumerate(xq_.shape)]
    freqs_cis = freqs_cis.view(*shape)
    
    # 3. Rotate via complex multiplication and flatten back to original dims
    xq_out = torch.view_as_real(xq_ * freqs_cis).flatten(3)
    xk_out = torch.view_as_real(xk_ * freqs_cis).flatten(3)
    
    # 4. Return in the original precision (e.g., bfloat16)
    return xq_out.type_as(xq), xk_out.type_as(xk)

title: Decoding RoPE: Rotary Positional Embeddings
section: Why the Industry Standardized on RoPE
text:
Why the Industry Standardized on RoPE

Relative Distance Guarantee: It mathematically forces the attention dot-product to care about relative distance, rather than absolute array indices.
Context Scaling: By dynamically manipulating the \(\theta\) base frequency (NTK scaling), we can stretch context windows to millions of tokens at inference time.
Persistent Signal: By injecting position directly into the attention mechanism at every layer, it eliminates the depth-induced signal dilution suffered by Layer 0 embeddings.
Linear Attention Compatibility: Because it is applied to the vectors before attention, it is natively compatible with memory-optimized kernels like FlashAttention.

By moving position out of the embedding space and into the geometric structure of the vector itself, RoPE solved the Transformer’s most stubborn scaling bottleneck.

title: BLEU Score: The Mathematics of Machine Translation
text:
Published by Ramu Nalla - January 01, 2026
In the world of supervised learning—like spam classification or customer churn prediction—evaluation is straightforward. The model is right, or it’s wrong. Accuracy, precision, and recall tell us everything we need to know.
But what happens when you are evaluating a machine translation model?
If the human reference translates a French sentence as “The cat sat on the mat,” but your model outputs “A cat was sitting on the mat,” is the model wrong? No. It conveys the same meaning. It just uses different words.
Language is inherently ambiguous. There is rarely one single “gold standard” translation. This subjectivity makes automated evaluation of Generative AI incredibly difficult.
For two decades, the de facto standard for solving this problem in Machine Translation (MT) has been the BLEU (Bilingual Evaluation Understudy) score. While newer semantic metrics like BERTScore are gaining traction, understanding BLEU is non-negotiable for any serious NLP practitioner. It is the bedrock upon which modern metrics are built.
In this post, we are going to tear apart the BLEU score. We won’t just look at the final formula; we will build it piece by piece to understand the mathematical intuition behind why it works—and crucial for us data scientists—where it breaks.

title: BLEU Score: The Mathematics of Machine Translation
section: The Core Intuition: Precision vs. Recall
text:
The Core Intuition: Precision vs. Recall
When evaluating generated text against a reference, we have two fundamental choices:

Precision: How much of what the model generated was correct?
Recall: How much of the reference did the model capture?

BLEU is fundamentally a Precision-based metric.
Why? Imagine a translation task where the goal is to translate a complex German technical document.

A high-recall model might output a messy 5-page document that definitely contains all the right information, but buries it in hallucinations and noise.
A high-precision model might output a shorter, 3-page document. It might miss a nuance or two, but everything it did generate is accurate and fluent.

In translation, we generally prefer the latter. We want the generated text to be high quality, even if it’s slightly incomplete.

title: BLEU Score: The Mathematics of Machine Translation
section: Step 1: The Trap of Raw Precision
text:
Step 1: The Trap of Raw Precision
Let’s start with the simplest possible approach: counting word matches.

Reference 1: “The cat is on the mat.”
Reference 2: “There is a cat on the mat.”

Now, imagine a poorly trained model generates this candidate:

Candidate: “the the the the the the”

If we calculate standard unigram (1-word) precision:

Total candidate words: 6
Number of matches: 6 (The word “the” appears in the references).
Precision: 6/6 = 100%

This is obviously a catastrophic failure. A model could game the system by finding the most common word in the reference language and repeating it endlessly.

title: BLEU Score: The Mathematics of Machine Translation
text:
Step 2: The Fix — Modified (Clipped) Precision



A visual representation of how BLEU calculates “Clipped Precision,” mapping candidate words to reference constraints.


To solve the repetition problem, BLEU introduces clipped precision.
The rule is simple: A word in the candidate sentence can only be counted as a match up to the maximum number of times it appears in a single reference sentence.
Let’s re-evaluate our bad candidate:

Candidate: “the the the the the the”
Reference Constraint: The word “the” appears a maximum of two times (in Reference 1).

The math changes:

Total candidate words: 6
Clipped matches: 2 (We count the first two “the”s, and discard the remaining four).
Clipped Precision: 2/6 = 33.3%

This is much more reasonable. The metric now punishes absurd repetition.
As shown in the image at the top of this post, think of the reference sentences as providing a limited “budget” for each word count. Once the candidate uses up that budget, subsequent uses of that word are worthless.

title: BLEU Score: The Mathematics of Machine Translation
text:
Step 3: Fluency and N-Grams
Matching individual words is good for ensuring the content is roughly adequate, but it doesn’t ensure the sentence flows naturally.
“Mat on is the cat the” contains all the right words, but it’s terrible English.
BLEU solves this by calculating clipped precision not just for unigrams (1-grams), but also for bigrams (2-grams), trigrams (3-grams), and typically up to 4-grams.

1-grams measure adequacy (are the right concepts there?).
3-grams and 4-grams measure fluency (is the phrasing natural?).

If a model gets high precision on 4-grams, it means it’s getting long sequences of words exactly right, which usually correlates with high-quality, fluent text.

Combining the Scores
So, we have four separate precision scores (\(p_1, p_2, p_3, p_4\)). How do we combine them into a single number?
We don’t take the arithmetic mean (an average). We take the geometric mean.


Why?
The geometric mean is highly sensitive to low scores. If a translation has excellent unigram precision (all the right words) but zero 4-gram precision (the order is completely scrambled), the geometric mean will crash toward zero. The arithmetic mean would still give it a decent pass. We want the metric to be harsh on models that fail at any level of granularity.
The combined precision score looks like this:
\[\exp\left( \sum_{n=1}^{N} w_n \log p_n \right)\]
Typically, \(N=4\) and the weights \(w_n\) are uniform (\(1/4\)).

title: BLEU Score: The Mathematics of Machine Translation
section: Step 4: The Brevity Penalty (Preventing the Gaming)
text:
Step 4: The Brevity Penalty (Preventing the Gaming)
We established that BLEU is a precision metric. But pure precision metrics have a massive loophole: Length.
If I have to translate a 50-word sentence, I could output a single, perfect 3-word phrase that I know is correct. My precision would be 100%. But I failed to translate the vast majority of the source sentence.
To prevent models from outputting overly short, “safe” sentences to maximize precision, BLEU applies a Brevity Penalty (BP).
The penalty is calculated based on two variables: 1. \(c\): The length of the candidate translation. 2. \(r\): The effective reference length.
The formula for BP is:
\[
BP = \begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } c > r \\
e^{(1 - r/c)} & \text{if } c \le r
\end{cases}
\]
If the candidate is longer than the reference (\(c > r\)), there is no penalty (\(BP = 1\)). We don’t punish the model for being verbose (that’s what the precision score already does if the extra words are wrong).
If the candidate is shorter than the reference (\(c \le r\)), the penalty kicks in exponentially.
Let’s visualize this.



A graph showing the BLEU Brevity Penalty. The Y-axis is the penalty multiplier (0 to 1), and the X-axis is the ratio of candidate length to reference length (c/r). The curve drops sharply below 1.0.


As you can see in the graph above, as soon as the candidate length drops below the reference length (ratio < 1.0), the score multiplier starts dropping from 1.0 toward 0.0. A candidate that is half the length of the reference gets heavily penalized, ensuring that high-BLEU systems must produce output of comparable length to humans.

title: BLEU Score: The Mathematics of Machine Translation
text:
The Complete Formula
Putting it all together, the BLEU score is the geometric mean of the n-gram clipped precisions, multiplied by the brevity penalty.
\[\textbf{BLEU} = \underbrace{BP}_{\text{Brevity Penalty}} \cdot \underbrace{\exp\left( \sum_{n=1}^{4} w_n \log p_n \right)}_{\text{Geometric Mean of N-Gram Precisions}}\]
The final score is between 0 and 1, though in practice, it is almost always reported as a percentage between 0 and 100.
A BLEU score over 30 is generally considered understandable. A score over 40-50 is often considered high quality, depending on the language pair (translating English to French is easier than English to Chinese, so expected scores vary).

title: BLEU Score: The Mathematics of Machine Translation
text:
Limitations
We’ve covered how BLEU works. Now, let’s talk about why it’s often frustrating. As data scientists, we need to know the limitations of our tools.
1. The Synonym Problem (Semantic Blindness). BLEU relies on exact string matching. It has no concept of meaning.

Reference: “The path is steep.”
Candidate: “The trail is uphill.”

BLEU will give this a very low score, despite it being a perfect translation. It punishes creative or varied vocabulary. This is why semantic embeddings-based metrics like BERTScore were invented.
2. It Ignores Global Structure While n-grams capture local ordering, BLEU has no sense of overall sentence structure. “Cat the sat mat on the” might get decent unigram scores, and it wouldn’t necessarily get a zero if some bigrams accidentally align.
3. Human Correlation is Wobbly Crucially, an increase in BLEU score does not always correspond to an increase in human-perceived quality. A model might game the metric by optimizing for 4-grams in a way that sounds mechanical to a human reader. BLEU is useful for tracking progress during training, but the final sanity check must always be human evaluation.
4. The Preprocessing Nightmare (sacrebleu) Because BLEU is based on exact string matching, it is incredibly sensitive to preprocessing. Do you tokenize text? Do you lowercase everything? How do you handle punctuation? Different implementations used to yield vastly different scores for the same model output.
Industry Standard: If you are reporting BLEU scores in a paper or a serious report, never roll your own implementation. Always use the sacrebleu Python library. It standardizes tokenization and preprocessing to ensure your scores are actually comparable to other people’s scores.

title: BLEU Score: The Mathematics of Machine Translation
text:
Final Thoughts
BLEU is imperfect. It is rigid, semantically blind, and sometimes fails to match human judgment.
However, it remains widely adopted in machine translation evaluation. It is fast to compute, easy to understand, and language-agnostic. Understanding the mechanics of clipped precision and the brevity penalty gives you the intuition needed to interpret these scores correctly—and to know when it’s time to move on to something more advanced.

title: Logit Dynamics - Controlling LLM Hallucinations with DoLa
text:
Published by Ramu Nalla - February 20, 2026
In my previous project, I focused on model adaptation—building LoRA from scratch to teach a model Elizabethan English. But as anyone building RAG systems knows, adapting a model is only half the battle. The other half is control.
Standard generation APIs treat LLMs as black boxes. You throw a prompt in, maybe tweak the temperature, and hope the model doesn’t confidently lie to your users. When hallucinations occur, the industry default is to blame the prompt or the training data.
For my latest project, Logit-Dynamics, I decided to look under the hood. I bypassed Hugging Face’s standard model.generate() API to build a custom PyTorch inference engine.
The goal? Expose the raw probability distributions, quantify the model’s uncertainty in real-time using Shannon Entropy, and actively suppress hallucinations using a 2023 technique called DoLa (Decoding by Contrasting Layers).

title: Logit Dynamics - Controlling LLM Hallucinations with DoLa
section: The Architecture: Intercepting the Forward Pass
text:
The Architecture: Intercepting the Forward Pass
To control an LLM, you have to understand that it doesn’t just output a single word; it outputs a massive vector of raw scores (logits) across its entire vocabulary.
Standard decoding strategies like Top-K or Nucleus (Top-p) Sampling operate on these final scores by simply chopping off the bottom of the probability distribution.

The Flaw in Standard Sampling
Top-K and Top-P prevent the model from picking completely random words from the “long tail.” But here is the critical flaw: “plausible-sounding lies” don’t live in the tail. They live right at the top of the distribution.
If a model is slightly unsure about a fact, the statistical bias of common words (like generic names or filler syntax) will artificially inflate their scores. If you blindly apply Top-K, you are essentially guaranteeing a confident hallucination.
To fix this, we need to look deeper into the Transformer’s reasoning hierarchy.

title: Logit Dynamics - Controlling LLM Hallucinations with DoLa
section: DoLa: Fast Thinkers vs. Deep Thinkers
text:
DoLa: Fast Thinkers vs. Deep Thinkers
Language models process information in stages:

Lower Layers (The Fast Thinker): Early layers focus on grammar, syntax, and statistical likelihood.
Higher Layers (The Deep Thinker): Later layers consolidate global context and retrieve factual knowledge.

DoLa fixes hallucinations by actively penalizing the “Fast Thinker.” It calculates the predictions at a mature layer, calculates them at a premature layer, and subtracts the difference.

The Contrastive Math
By contrasting the logits, we isolate the pure knowledge signal from the statistical noise:
\[S(y_t) = z_{\text{mature}} - \alpha \cdot z_{\text{premature}}\]
If a token has a high probability in the mature layer but a low probability in the premature layer, its score goes up (factual realization). If a token has a high probability in both layers, its score goes down (generic syntax).


The Implementation
Here is how I implemented the DoLa intervention in PyTorch:
def get_dola_logits(self, input_text, mature_layer=None, premature_layer=None, alpha=1.0):
    inputs = self.tokenizer(input_text, return_tensors="pt").to(self.device)
    
    # Forward pass requesting ALL hidden states
    with torch.no_grad():
        outputs = self.model(**inputs, output_hidden_states=True)
        
    # Extract the hidden state for the LAST token
    mature_hidden = outputs.hidden_states[mature_layer][0, -1, :]
    premature_hidden = outputs.hidden_states[premature_layer][0, -1, :]
    
    # Project hidden states to Vocabulary Logits
    mature_logits = self.model.lm_head(mature_hidden)
    premature_logits = self.model.lm_head(premature_hidden)
    
    mature_probs = F.softmax(mature_logits, dim=-1)
    
    # Dynamic threshold: Only consider plausible tokens
    top_k_probs, _ = torch.topk(mature_probs, 50)
    threshold = top_k_probs[-1] 
    
    # Contrastive Subtraction
    contrastive_logits = mature_logits - (alpha * premature_logits)
    
    # Mask out the noise 
    mask = mature_probs < threshold
    contrastive_logits[mask] = -float('inf')
    
    dola_probs = F.softmax(contrastive_logits, dim=-1)
    return dola_probs

title: Logit Dynamics - Controlling LLM Hallucinations with DoLa
text:
The Hallucination Monitor: Shannon Entropy
Fixing the logits is powerful, but in an MLOps context, telemetry is just as important. How do we know when the model is struggling?
When an LLM is reciting a known fact, its probability distribution is sharp. When it is guessing, the distribution flattens out. I quantified this uncertainty at every single generation step using Shannon Entropy.
\[H(X) = - \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i) \ln P(x_i)\]
def calculate_shannon_entropy(probs, epsilon=1e-9):
    """
    High Entropy = Flat Distribution = High Hallucination Risk.
    Low Entropy = Sharp Distribution = High Confidence.
    """
    entropy = -torch.sum(probs * torch.log(probs + epsilon))
    return entropy.item()
By tracking this metric over time, we can create a literal lie detector for the model.

title: Logit Dynamics - Controlling LLM Hallucinations with DoLa
text:
The Logit-Dynamics Observatory
To bring this all together, I built a custom Streamlit dashboard that visualizes this data in real-time.
 

The Heatmap
The app dynamically injects HTML to color-code the generated text. Green indicates low entropy (confidence). When the model starts guessing—often right before generating a fake entity or hallucinated API call—the text bleeds red.


The Token Inspector
The dashboard allows you to scrub through the generated sequence. You can select any individual token and view the exact Top-15 probability landscape at that precise microsecond, watching how DoLa re-ranked the options to save the model from a hallucination loop.

title: Logit Dynamics - Controlling LLM Hallucinations with DoLa
text:
Final Thoughts
By breaking open the inference loop, writing the sampling math from first principles, and applying contrastive layer decoding, we can engineer truth directly into the generation process.
Check out the full repository and mathematical breakdowns on my GitHub.

title: KV Cache: From O(n²) to O(n) in LLM Inference
text:
Published by Ramu Nalla - April 25, 2026
Scaling language model inference fundamentally comes down to battling two major bottlenecks: Compute (FLOPs) and Memory Bandwidth (VRAM).
When a Transformer generates text, it does so autoregressively—one token at a time. The naive approach to this generation is mathematically elegant but computationally disastrous. If we ask a model to generate the 100th token, the naive approach recalculates the attention scores for tokens 1 through 99 all over again.
The KV Cache (Key-Value Cache) is the industry-standard architectural hack that solves this compute bottleneck. By caching the intermediate mathematical states of past tokens, we reduce the time complexity of generation from a quadratically growing \(O(n^2)\) nightmare into a flat, predictable \(O(n)\) operation.
In this post, I will walk through the concept, the foundational math, a from-scratch PyTorch implementation, and a deep dive into the hardware-level profiling of what actually happens in VRAM when we turn the cache on versus off.

title: KV Cache: From O(n²) to O(n) in LLM Inference
section: 1. The Math: Why Autoregressive Generation is Slow
text:
1. The Math: Why Autoregressive Generation is Slow

The Attention Mechanism
In a standard Transformer, the core dot-product attention equation is:
\[\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^T}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)V\]
During the Prefill Phase (processing the initial prompt), the model reads the entire sequence in parallel. The Queries (\(Q\)), Keys (\(K\)), and Values (\(V\)) are all \(N \times d\) matrices (where \(N\) is sequence length and \(d\) is embedding dimension). The \(QK^T\) multiplication results in an \(N \times N\) attention matrix.


The Generation Bottleneck (No Cache)
During the Decoding Phase, the model predicts the next token. If we do not use a cache, the input sequence length grows by 1 every step.

To generate token 100, we pass an input of size \(100\). The model computes a \(100 \times 100\) attention matrix.
To generate token 101, we pass an input of size \(101\). The model computes a \(101 \times 101\) attention matrix.

The model is redundantly re-computing the Keys and Values for tokens 1 through 100. This results in \(O(n^2)\) time complexity. As \(N\) grows, the compute required to generate a single word skyrockets.


The KV Cache Solution
The mathematical realization behind the KV cache is that for a newly generated token, its Query vector only needs to attend to the Key vectors of past tokens. The past tokens’ Keys and Values do not change.
If we store (cache) the \(K\) and \(V\) tensors of all previous tokens in memory:

The new input is just the single newly generated token (\(1 \times d\)).
We project this single token into a \(1 \times d\) Query, Key, and Value.
We append the new \(1 \times d\) Key and Value to our cached history.
We multiply the \(1 \times d\) Query against the \(N \times d\) cached Keys.

The resulting attention matrix is \(1 \times N\). The compute time drops to \(O(n)\), and latency flattens out entirely.

title: KV Cache: From O(n²) to O(n) in LLM Inference
text:
2. Minimal PyTorch Implementation
To see this in action, I built a stripped-down Attention module and a state manager to route the memory.

The Cache State Manager
This module intercepts the Keys and Values for the newly generated token and forcefully concatenates them to the existing history.
import torch
from typing import Tuple, Optional

class StandardKVCache:
    """
    Implements a standard, contiguous KV-Cache.
    Tensors grow linearly with each generation step.
    """
    def __init__(self):
        self.k_cache: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None
        self.v_cache: Optional[torch.Tensor] = None

    def update(self, k_new: torch.Tensor, v_new: torch.Tensor) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        """Appends new keys and values to the contiguous cache."""
        if self.k_cache is None or self.v_cache is None:
            self.k_cache = k_new
            self.v_cache = v_new
        else:
            # Concatenate along the sequence length dimension (dim=2)
            self.k_cache = torch.cat([self.k_cache, k_new], dim=2)
            self.v_cache = torch.cat([self.v_cache, v_new], dim=2)
            
        return self.k_cache, self.v_cache

    def reset(self):
        self.k_cache = None
        self.v_cache = None


The Model Backbone
This implements the core attention math, conditionally routing it through the cache. Notice the torch.cat bottleneck under the hood.
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math

class MinimalAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, embed_dim=256, num_heads=8):
        super().__init__()
        self.num_heads = num_heads
        self.head_dim = embed_dim // num_heads
        
        self.q_proj = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.k_proj = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.v_proj = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)
        self.out_proj = nn.Linear(embed_dim, embed_dim)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor, kv_cache: StandardKVCache = None) -> torch.Tensor:
        batch_size, seq_len, embed_dim = x.shape
        
        # 1. Project Q, K, V
        q = self.q_proj(x).view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = self.k_proj(x).view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = self.v_proj(x).view(batch_size, seq_len, self.num_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)

        # 2. KV-Cache Routing
        if kv_cache is not None:
            # Standard Cache active: append new K, V and fetch full history
            k, v = kv_cache.update(k, v)
        
        # 3. Core Attention Math
        scores = torch.matmul(q, k.transpose(-2, -1)) / math.sqrt(self.head_dim)
        
        if kv_cache is None and seq_len > 1:
            mask = torch.tril(torch.ones(seq_len, seq_len, device=x.device)).view(1, 1, seq_len, seq_len)
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float('-inf'))
            
        attn_weights = torch.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        out = torch.matmul(attn_weights, v)
        
        # 4. Reassemble and project
        out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(batch_size, seq_len, embed_dim)
        return self.out_proj(out)


The Profiling Harness
To prove the math, we run a 1000-step autoregressive loop, tracking the Time-Per-Output-Token (TPOT).
import torch
import time
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def simulate_inference(attention_layer, prefix_len, gen_steps, use_cache=False, device='cpu'):
    embed_dim = 256
    latencies = []
    
    # Context Phase (Prefill)
    prefix_tokens = torch.randn(1, prefix_len, embed_dim, device=device)
    cache = StandardKVCache() if use_cache else None
    
    start_time = time.perf_counter()
    _ = attention_layer(prefix_tokens, kv_cache=cache)
    ttft = time.perf_counter() - start_time
    
    # Generation Phase
    current_seq_len = prefix_len
    
    for _ in range(gen_steps):
        step_start = time.perf_counter()
        
        if use_cache:
            next_token = torch.randn(1, 1, embed_dim, device=device)
            _ = attention_layer(next_token, kv_cache=cache)
        else:
            current_seq_len += 1
            full_sequence = torch.randn(1, current_seq_len, embed_dim, device=device)
            _ = attention_layer(full_sequence, kv_cache=None)
            
        step_time = time.perf_counter() - step_start
        latencies.append(step_time * 1000)
        
    return ttft * 1000, latencies

# ... (Execution wrapper omitted for brevity) ...

title: KV Cache: From O(n²) to O(n) in LLM Inference
section: 3. Hardware Realities: Reading the Profiler Plot
text:
3. Hardware Realities: Reading the Profiler Plot
When we execute the above code on hardware (in this case, an Apple Silicon Mac via the MPS backend), the resulting latency plot reveals a story that is much messier than the theoretical \(O(n^2)\) math.





Aggregate timings from the run (prefix prefill for TTFT, then 1000 decode steps; same harness as the code above, MPS on Apple Silicon):





TTFT (ms)
Avg TPOT (ms)




No cache
220.12
65.29


With cache
5.11
6.20





Average TPOT (time per output token) is the average time to produce one new token during generation. Here: 65.29 ms without a cache versus 6.20 ms with it—about 10× faster with the cache, because each step reuses stored keys and values instead of recomputing attention over the full sequence so far.
TTFT (time to first token) is how long the initial prefill takes before the first token is ready. Here: 220.12 ms without a cache versus 5.11 ms with it—about 43× faster with the cache, because the cached path does not pay the same one-shot cost before decode can start.


The “Memory Quake” (The 7000ms Spike)
Around step 520, the No Cache (red) line hits a massive 7-second latency wall. Why? Because the bottleneck here isn’t actually the FLOPs required to multiply the matrices—it is PyTorch’s Caching Memory Allocator.
In the “No Cache” loop, we ask the hardware to calculate a continuously growing \(N \times N\) attention grid. At step 520, the operating system ran out of readily available contiguous VRAM. It had to “stop the world,” trigger garbage collection, defragment the memory, and allocate a massive new contiguous block before inference could resume.


The Post-Spike Drop
Notice how the red line’s baseline latency actually drops and flattens out after the 7000ms spike. Because PyTorch successfully negotiated a massive, fresh block of VRAM during that pause, the subsequent hundreds of steps no longer suffer from constant OS-level memory allocation overhead. The latency becomes purely compute-bound, proving that in LLM inference, memory management often dictates speed more than raw compute capability.


The Flatline of the KV Cache
The blue line (Standard KV-Cache) bypasses the \(N \times N\) grid entirely, maintaining a rock-solid \(\sim6\text{ms}\) latency. The memory allocator handles the tiny \(1 \times N\) requests effortlessly.

title: KV Cache: From O(n²) to O(n) in LLM Inference
text:
Conclusion
The KV Cache perfectly solves the compute scaling problem of autoregressive generation. However, it is not a silver bullet. As we saw in our code, the StandardKVCache relies on torch.cat, meaning it continuously requests linearly larger blocks of contiguous memory.
While it solves the compute bottleneck, the standard KV cache introduces a massive memory capacity bottleneck: VRAM fragmentation. Pre-allocating contiguous VRAM for maximum sequence lengths wastes incredible amounts of memory, which is exactly why modern inference engines have abandoned this approach in favor of advanced OS-style block memory management (PagedAttention).

title: PagedAttention: Breaking the KV Cache Memory Wall
text:
Published by Ramu Nalla - April 26, 2026
Deploying Agentic AI systems and scaling LLMs exposes a brutal reality of MLOps: compute is rarely your actual bottleneck—memory is. Specifically, VRAM capacity dictates how many concurrent users or agents you can serve.
In autoregressive generation, we cache the representations of past tokens so we don’t have to recompute them. This is the KV Cache. Historically, AI frameworks treated the KV Cache like any standard tensor: it had to live in a single, contiguous block of VRAM. This strict requirement created massive memory fragmentation and bottlenecked throughput.
PagedAttention is the breakthrough that solved this by borrowing a classic concept from operating systems: virtual memory paging.
In this post, I will break down the limitations of the traditional KV cache, explain how PagedAttention fixes it using Block Tables, walk through a real-world analogy, follow one toy generation request step by step, and share a simplified PyTorch mental model of the forward pass.

title: PagedAttention: Breaking the KV Cache Memory Wall
section: 1. The Problem: Traditional KV Cache Allocation
text:
1. The Problem: Traditional KV Cache Allocation
When an LLM generates text, it reads the prompt and outputs one token at a time. To avoid recalculating the Key (K) and Value (V) vectors for every previous token at every single step, we store them in the KV Cache.

The Contiguous Memory Rule
Before PagedAttention (in vanilla PyTorch or HuggingFace inference), the KV cache tensor was subjected to standard matrix physics: tensors require contiguous memory.
Because sequence lengths are highly unpredictable (you never know if an agent will generate a “Yes” or a 2,000-word essay), the inference engine had a terrible choice to make:

Guess small: Risk running out of memory (OOM) mid-generation, crashing the request.
Over-reserve: Pre-allocate a massive, contiguous chunk of VRAM equal to the model’s maximum context length for every single request.



The Memory Wall (Examples of Waste)
Pre-allocation causes catastrophic memory waste through two mechanisms:

Internal Fragmentation: You pre-allocate space for 2,048 tokens. The user asks a simple question, and the LLM responds with 18 tokens. The remaining 2,030 token slots are locked, sitting empty, and completely unusable by other requests.
External Fragmentation: Over time, VRAM becomes a checkerboard of used and empty spaces. You might have 5GB of total free VRAM, but because it is broken up into tiny gaps between active tensors, you cannot find a single 2GB contiguous block to start a new request.

The Result: Studies showed that in standard inference servers, up to 80% of the KV Cache VRAM was wasted.

title: PagedAttention: Breaking the KV Cache Memory Wall
text:
2. The Solution: PagedAttention
PagedAttention removes the requirement for contiguous memory. It breaks the KV cache into small, fixed-size chunks called blocks (typically holding 16 or 32 tokens).

A Real-Life Analogy: The Restaurant Seating Problem

The Traditional KV Cache: Imagine a restaurant that only has massive, 20-person banquet tables. If a couple walks in for dinner, the host seats them at a 20-person table. 18 chairs are completely wasted. If another couple walks in, they cannot sit at the empty chairs of the first table; they need their own giant 20-person table. The restaurant is “full” after seating just a few people.
PagedAttention: The restaurant uses small, 4-person tables that can be logically grouped. When a couple walks in, they get one 4-person table. If a party of 10 walks in, the host gives them three 4-person tables scattered wherever there is space across the room. The waiter (the Block Table) simply remembers which scattered tables belong to which party and serves them in order. The restaurant can now operate at near 100% capacity.



How it Works Under the Hood

Logical vs. Physical: To the Transformer’s math operations, the sequence still looks like one long, continuous (logical) sequence. In reality, it is scattered across non-contiguous (physical) blocks in VRAM.
The Block Table: PagedAttention maintains a translation map called the Block Table. It records which physical VRAM blocks belong to which logical sequence.
Dynamic Allocation: When an agent starts generating text, the engine allocates just one small physical block. Once that block fills up with tokens, it allocates a new one anywhere in VRAM and updates the Block Table.

title: PagedAttention: Breaking the KV Cache Memory Wall
section: 3. Walkthrough: One Request, Step by Step
text:
3. Walkthrough: One Request, Step by Step
The restaurant analogy explains the idea; a concrete token run shows how the Block Table and physical blocks move in lockstep. Production systems often use blocks of 16 or 32 tokens. For readability, this walkthrough assumes block size 4—the bookkeeping is identical, just with smaller numbers.
Suppose one user sends a short prompt and the model will decode two new tokens afterward.

Prompt (prefill): ["The", " quick", " brown", " fox"]
Target generation (decode): [" jumps", " over"]


Step 1: Prefill—processing the prompt
Before anything new is generated, the engine must ingest the prompt.
Computation. The Transformer processes all four prompt tokens together (this is the prefill phase). For each token it computes the Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) vectors.
Allocation. The scheduler looks at its pool of physical VRAM blocks and picks an empty one—say physical block 12.
Storage. It writes the K and V entries for all four prompt tokens into that block. With block size 4, the first logical block is now full.
Block table. For this request, the engine records:

Logical block 0 \(\rightarrow\) physical block 12 (slots used: 4/4)

So far, this looks like ordinary caching—except the “row” in VRAM is exactly one small block, not a giant pre-allocated tensor for max context length.


Step 2: First decode token—crossing a block boundary
Now the model enters the autoregressive loop and produces the first new token.
Generation. Given the prompt context, the language head predicts the next token: " jumps".
Computation. The model computes K and V for " jumps". Those vectors must be stored in the KV cache for every later step.
Why allocate again? The block table says logical block 0 maps to physical block 12, and that block is already 4/4. There is no room for another token in that physical page, so the engine must grab a new physical block. Suppose free space shows up at physical block 3.
Storage. The K and V tensors for " jumps" go into the first slot of physical block 3.
Block table after this step:

Logical block 0 \(\rightarrow\) physical block 12 (slots used: 4/4)
Logical block 1 \(\rightarrow\) physical block 3 (slots used: 1/4)

Notice the sequence is still logically one continuous string of five tokens; physically, the first four live in block 12 and the fifth lives at the start of block 3.


Step 3: Second decode token—where PagedAttention shows up in the math
The model now continues from the full context ["The", " quick", " brown", " fox", " jumps"] and must emit the next token.
Query. From the current position (the token " jumps"), the model computes the Q vector for the next attention step.
Attention. To form attention scores, that Q must interact with the K vectors of all prior tokens—four from the prompt plus " jumps".
Fetch (the important part). A naive layout would assume one contiguous K tensor in VRAM. PagedAttention does not. The custom kernel consults the block table and issues the reads it actually needs—for example:

Go to physical block 12 and read four K/V slots (the prompt).
Jump to physical block 3 and read one K/V slot (" jumps").

The softmax and the weighted sum over values still run on the same mathematical sequence; only the memory access pattern is paged.
Generation. After attention and the feed-forward layers, the model predicts " over".
Storage. K and V for " over" are computed. Logical block 1 still has three free slots in physical block 3, so no new physical block is required—the new vectors simply occupy the second slot in block 3.
Block table after this step:

Logical block 0 \(\rightarrow\) physical block 12 (slots used: 4/4)
Logical block 1 \(\rightarrow\) physical block 3 (slots used: 2/4)



The system-level view
While this one request was using physical blocks 12 and 3, other users’ sequences are decoding in parallel, filling blocks 1, 2, 4, 13, and so on. The allocator hands out small pages wherever they fit; the block table per request is what preserves the illusion of a single contiguous KV tensor for attention math.
Treating the KV cache like virtual memory—logical order mapped to scattered physical pages—is how the engine packs VRAM tightly without pre-allocating one enormous contiguous tensor per request or repeatedly forcing expensive torch.cat-style reallocations when sequences grow.

title: PagedAttention: Breaking the KV Cache Memory Wall
text:
4. A Conceptual PyTorch Implementation
You cannot use a standard torch.matmul(Q, K.T) on scattered memory. PagedAttention requires custom CUDA/Triton kernels to read the Block Table and fetch the scattered chunks during the attention computation.
Here is a simplified PyTorch pseudo-code block to illustrate how this logic looks inside the inference loop.
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def paged_attention_forward(
    query: torch.Tensor,               # (num_seqs, num_heads, head_size)
    key_cache: torch.Tensor,           # (num_blocks, block_size, num_heads, head_size)
    value_cache: torch.Tensor,         # (num_blocks, block_size, num_heads, head_size)
    block_tables: torch.Tensor,        # (num_seqs, max_blocks_per_seq) -> Maps sequence to physical blocks
    context_lens: torch.Tensor,        # (num_seqs) -> How many tokens in each sequence
    block_size: int
) -> torch.Tensor:
    
    num_seqs, num_heads, head_size = query.shape
    out = torch.zeros_like(query)

    # In reality, this loop is aggressively parallelized in a custom CUDA kernel
    for i in range(num_seqs):
        seq_len = context_lens[i].item()
        
        # 1. Fetch exactly which physical blocks this sequence is scattered across
        num_blocks_needed = (seq_len + block_size - 1) // block_size
        physical_block_indices = block_tables[i, :num_blocks_needed]
        
        # 2. Gather the scattered Key and Value chunks into temporary contiguous tensors
        # (A real CUDA kernel does the dot product directly on the fly without this copy step!)
        K_seq = key_cache[physical_block_indices]    # (blocks, block_size, heads, dim)
        V_seq = value_cache[physical_block_indices]
        
        # Flatten blocks to look like a normal sequence: (seq_len, heads, dim)
        K_seq = K_seq.view(-1, num_heads, head_size)[:seq_len]
        V_seq = V_seq.view(-1, num_heads, head_size)[:seq_len]
        
        # 3. Standard Attention Math for this specific sequence
        q_i = query[i].unsqueeze(0) # (1, heads, dim)
        
        # Transpose for dot product: Q @ K^T
        scores = torch.einsum('qhd,khd->qhk', q_i, K_seq) / (head_size ** 0.5)
        attn_weights = F.softmax(scores, dim=-1)
        
        # Weighted sum of values: Weights @ V
        out[i] = torch.einsum('qhk,khd->qhd', attn_weights, V_seq).squeeze(0)

    return out
Note: The real vLLM implementation does not actually gather and copy the memory into a contiguous K_seq tensor before computing attention—doing so would trigger the exact memory bottleneck we are trying to avoid! Instead, the custom kernel performs the dot-product computations chunk-by-chunk directly from the scattered physical blocks.

title: PagedAttention: Breaking the KV Cache Memory Wall
text:
5. The Impact on Modern Architectures
PagedAttention fundamentally changed LLM serving:

Massive Batch Sizes: By eliminating VRAM waste, a single GPU can hold the KV cache for significantly more concurrent requests.
Continuous Batching: Because memory is dynamically assigned block-by-block, engines can safely inject new requests into the GPU at the exact millisecond an old request finishes, without waiting for the whole batch to complete.
Agentic State Sharing: If multiple agents share the same long system prompt, PagedAttention allows them to physically share the same memory blocks for that prompt, only diverging when they generate unique text.

title: PagedAttention: Breaking the KV Cache Memory Wall
text:
6. The Drawbacks (The Engineering Reality)
While PagedAttention is the gold standard for throughput, it introduces specific trade-offs:

Loss of Coalesced Memory Access: GPUs are fastest when sweeping through unbroken, contiguous arrays. By forcing the GPU to jump around VRAM fetching scattered blocks, we take a slight penalty to pure computational speed compared to static pre-allocation.
Internal Block Fragmentation: We still waste a tiny bit of memory. If your block size is 16 tokens and a sequence stops at 17 tokens, you must allocate two full blocks. Those remaining 15 slots in the second block are wasted.
CPU Bookkeeping Overhead: Pre-allocation requires one memory operation at the start of a sequence. PagedAttention requires the CPU to actively monitor block capacity, hunt for free VRAM blocks, update the Block Table, and sync with the GPU at every generation step.
Kernel Complexity: You cannot just call torch.compile on PagedAttention. It requires maintaining highly complex, low-level custom kernels that must be re-written for different hardware architectures (NVIDIA, AMD, Apple Silicon).

title: PagedAttention: Breaking the KV Cache Memory Wall
text:
Conclusion
PagedAttention proves that scaling machine learning systems isn’t just about training larger parameter models; it is about relentlessly optimizing the MLOps infrastructure that serves them. By breaking the contiguous memory rule, we traded a small amount of engineering complexity and CPU overhead for a massive leap in concurrent serving capacity.

title: Deep Dive into Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)
text:
Published by Ramu Nalla - January 29, 2026
The promise of Large Language Models is universal capability. The reality is that they are heavy.
Fine-tuning a model like Llama-3 (8B) or even the smaller DistilGPT2 (82M) traditionally meant Full Fine-Tuning (FFT): updating every single weight in the network. For an 8B model, this requires storing 8 billion gradients and 8 billion optimizer states (Adam carries two states per parameter). This quickly exceeds the VRAM of even high-end consumer GPUs.
But do we really need to update all 8 billion parameters to teach a model a new writing style?
In this post, I am going to deconstruct LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation). We won’t just look at the high-level concept; we will implement the mathematics in PyTorch, calculate the exact parameter savings, and solve specific engineering hurdles like adapting GPT-2’s Conv1D layers and stabilizing inference loops.

title: Deep Dive into Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)
text:
1. Mathematics

The Hypothesis: Intrinsic Rank
LoRA is founded on a hypothesis from the paper Intrinsic Dimensionality Explains the Effectiveness of Language Model Fine-Tuning (Aghajanyan et al., 2020). It states that pre-trained models have a very low “intrinsic dimension.” The changes required to adapt them to a new task (\(\Delta W\)) exist in a low-rank subspace.


The Decomposition
Let \(W_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times k}\) be a frozen pre-trained weight matrix. Instead of learning the full update matrix \(\Delta W\) (which would also be \(d \times k\)), we decompose it into the product of two much smaller matrices:
\[W = W_0 + \Delta W = W_0 + B A\]
Where:

\(A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times k}\): The “Down-projection” matrix.
\(B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r}\): The “Up-projection” matrix.
\(r\): The rank (hyperparameter), typically \(r \ll \min(d, k)\).

For example, if \(W_0\) is \(1024 \times 1024\) (\(d=1024\)):

Full Fine-Tuning: We train \(1,048,576\) parameters.
LoRA (\(r=8\)):

\(A\): \(8 \times 1024 = 8,192\) params.
\(B\): \(1024 \times 8 = 8,192\) params.
Total: \(16,384\) parameters.


This is a 98.4% reduction in trainable parameters for that single layer.


The Forward Pass & Scaling
The modified forward pass for an input \(x\) becomes:
\[h = W_0 x + \frac{\alpha}{r} (B A x)\]
Notice the term \(\frac{\alpha}{r}\). This is the scaling factor.

\(\alpha\) (Alpha): A constant that determines the strength of the adapter.
Why scale? It decouples the hyperparameter search. If you tune your learning rate for \(r=8\), scaling by \(1/r\) ensures you don’t need to re-tune the learning rate if you try \(r=16\) or \(r=4\).
Without \(\alpha\), if you increased the rank from 8 to 64, the “signal” coming out of the LoRA matrices would become much stronger, potentially blowing up your gradients or overshooting the local minima.
With \(\alpha\), because it is part of the fraction \(\frac{\alpha}{r}\), increasing the rank automatically decreases the scaling factor. This means you can experiment with different ranks (\(r=8, 16, 32, 64\)) without having to restart your hyperparameter search for the perfect learning rate every single time. If you decide to increase the rank later to capture more complex patterns, the \(\frac{\alpha}{r}\) term will keep the “magnitude” of your updates consistent, so your training remains stable

title: Deep Dive into Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)
text:
2. Implementation of The LoRALinear Layer
Implementing this in PyTorch requires wrapping an existing nn.Linear layer. We must ensure the original weights remain frozen while \(A\) and \(B\) are trainable.
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import math

class LoRALinear(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, base_layer: nn.Linear, rank: int = 8, alpha: int = 16):
        super().__init__()
        # 1. Capture the existing weights
        self.base_layer = base_layer
        
        # 2. Define LoRA Hyperparameters
        self.rank = rank
        self.scaling = alpha / rank
        
        # 3. Extract Dimensions
        # Note: In PyTorch Linear, weights are stored as (out_features, in_features)
        self.in_features = base_layer.in_features
        self.out_features = base_layer.out_features
        
        # 4. Initialize A and B
        # Matrix A: (rank, in_features) -> Down projection
        self.lora_A = nn.Parameter(torch.randn(rank, self.in_features))
        # Matrix B: (out_features, rank) -> Up projection
        self.lora_B = nn.Parameter(torch.zeros(self.out_features, rank))
        
        # 5. Freeze the Base Layer
        self.base_layer.weight.requires_grad = False
        if self.base_layer.bias is not None:
            self.base_layer.bias.requires_grad = False
            
        # 6. Reset Parameters (Crucial Step)
        self.reset_parameters()

    def reset_parameters(self):
        # Initialize A with Kaiming Uniform (standard for Relu/Linear networks)
        nn.init.kaiming_uniform_(self.lora_A, a=math.sqrt(5))
        
        # Initialize B with ZEROS
        # This ensures that at step 0, BAx = 0.
        # The model starts exactly as the pre-trained model.
        nn.init.zeros_(self.lora_B)

    def forward(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        # Standard Path (Frozen)
        result = self.base_layer(x)
        
        # LoRA Path (Trainable)
        # Equation: (x @ A^T) @ B^T * scaling
        # Note: PyTorch linear uses x @ W^T, so we transpose A and B
        lora_out = (x @ self.lora_A.t()) @ self.lora_B.t()
        
        return result + (lora_out * self.scaling)

Why Initialize B to Zero?
This is a critical engineering detail. If we initialized both \(A\) and \(B\) randomly, the untrained adapter would inject random noise into the forward pass before training even began. This would degrade the model’s perplexity instantly. By setting \(B=0\), the initial product \(B \times A\) is zero, preserving the pre-trained behavior perfectly at step 0.

title: Deep Dive into Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)
section: 3. Architecture: Where Does LoRA Actually Live?
text:
3. Architecture: Where Does LoRA Actually Live?
When we say a model like GPT-2 Small has 124 million parameters, we are essentially saying it stores 124 million individual decimal numbers (weights) in its memory. If you were to open the model’s file, you would see giant grids (matrices) of floating-point numbers.
To understand exactly where LoRA injects itself, we need a map of these 124M parameters. Here is the anatomical breakdown of a standard Transformer block.

A. The Embedding Layer (~39M params)
Before the model thinks, it converts words into numbers.

What it is: A giant lookup table.
The Matrix: If the vocabulary is 50,257 words and the hidden size is 768, this is a matrix of shape [50,257, 768].
LoRA Impact: We typically do not apply LoRA here. These weights remain frozen.



B. The Transformer Blocks (The Core)
This is where the logic happens. A 124M model stacks 12 identical blocks. Each block has two main “rooms” where parameters live, and this is where we apply LoRA.

1. The Attention Room (c_attn)
This is the most common target for LoRA (target_modules=["c_attn"]).

The Weights: This layer holds the matrices for Query, Key, and Value.
The Shape: For a hidden size of 768, the c_attn layer is typically one giant matrix of [768, 2304] (since \(768 \times 3 = 2304\) - Query, Key and Values).
Parameter Count: \(768 \times 2304 \approx \mathbf{1.7 \text{ million parameters}}\) per block.
LoRA Strategy: We freeze these 1.7M numbers. We then inject an Adapter (\(A\) and \(B\)) that approximates the changes to this layer.



2. The Feed-Forward Room (mlp)
After each attention layer, data flows through two dense layers.

The Weights: One layer expands the data (768 \(\to\) 3072) and the second shrinks it back (3072 \(\to\) 768).
Parameter Count: This is actually the “heaviest” part of the block, containing roughly 4.7 million parameters.
LoRA Strategy: While original LoRA papers focused on Attention, modern implementations (and DoRA) often target these MLP layers as well to capture more stylistic nuance.




C. The Final Head (~39M params)
At the very top, the model converts its internal thought vector (768) back into a word prediction (50,257 options).

LoRA Impact: Usually skipped to save parameters, though some fine-tuning strategies target this for vocabulary adaptation.

title: Deep Dive into Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)
text:
4. Parameter Reduction Analysis
Let’s look at the actual numbers from my DistilGPT2 project. I targeted the Attention Query, Key, Value projection layers.
Base Model Layer (c_attn):

Input dim: 768
Output dim: 2304 (\(3 \times 768\) for Q, K, V)
Parameters: \(768 \times 2304 = \mathbf{1,769,472}\)

LoRA Adapter (\(r=8\)):

Matrix A: \(8 \times 768 = 6,144\)
Matrix B: \(2304 \times 8 = 18,432\)
Parameters: \(\mathbf{24,576}\)

Reduction Factor: \[1 - \frac{24,576}{1,769,472} \approx 98.6\%\]
Across the entire model, we reduced the trainable count from 82 Million to just ~235,000.

title: Deep Dive into Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)
section: 5. Engineering Scars: What Papers Don’t Tell You
text:
5. Engineering Scars: What Papers Don’t Tell You
Implementing LoRA is one thing; training it is another. Here are two major technical hurdles I encountered.

A. The Learning Rate Gap
